questions.question: What percentage of people report feeling disrespected by poor digital communication etiquette?
questions.explanation: 65% of people report feeling disrespected by poor digital communication etiquette including late responses, inappropriate medium choices, tone mismatches, and phubbing (phone-snubbing during conversations). With the average person switching between 7+ communication platforms daily—each with different response expectations, formality levels, and appropriateness rules—understanding netiquette prevents misunderstandings and signals respect in modern connection.

questions.question: According to media richness theory, what is the appropriate medium for emotionally sensitive or complex discussions?

options.text: Phone call or video for vocal tone and facial cues
Social media comments for public accountability

questions.explanation: Media richness theory matches communication medium to message complexity: high stakes/emotion/complexity = richer medium (call/video); simple/non-urgent = leaner medium (text/email). Difficult/emotional topics require voice or face because vocal tone and facial cues communicate empathy that text cannot—'We need to talk' via text about serious topic creates anxiety and misinterpretation. Example: friend's parent dies → call to express condolences, not just text, because richer medium shows care through time investment and empathic tone.

questions.question: What percentage of Americans admit to 'phubbing' (phone-snubbing)—checking phones during in-person conversations?
questions.explanation: 89% of Americans admit to phubbing—checking phones during in-person conversations—damaging connection quality. Even unintentionally, visible phone signals 'I'm monitoring for more important things than you.' Device-free time strategies like phone stacking (everyone places phones face-down, first to check pays bill), tech-free zones (bedroom, dinner table), and notification management create presence that deepens conversations, improves memory formation, reduces anxiety, and strengthens relationships by showing 'you have my full attention and matter to me.'

questions.question: To prevent Zoom fatigue during video calls, what is the recommended meeting length and buffer strategy?

options.text: 50-minute meetings allowing 10-minute buffer before next call

questions.explanation: Schedule 50-minute meetings (not 60) allowing 10-minute buffer before the next call to prevent back-to-back video exhaustion. This buffer allows bathroom breaks, movement, and mental resets preventing burnout. Additional strategies: hide self-view (reduces self-monitoring anxiety), make camera optional for casual meetings (audio-only reduces fatigue), look at camera when speaking for eye contact simulation, and suggest 5-minute breaks every 45-60 minutes for long meetings. Zoom fatigue results from cognitive load of processing non-verbal cues on screens and constant self-monitoring.

questions.question: If you find social media use increasing your loneliness and depression (comparing yourself to others' highlight reels, endless scrolling replacing real connection, feeling inadequate), what is the evidence-based strategy?

options.text: Scroll more to stay connected to everyone's lives
Accept loneliness as inevitable in the digital age
Set app time limits, notice comparison triggers and STOP scrolling when inadequacy emerges, use social media to COORDINATE in-person connection (not replace it)
Post more to get validation from likes and comments

questions.explanation: Research shows social media use correlates with increased loneliness and depression when it REPLACES real connection—but can enhance relationships when used to COORDINATE in-person meetups. Healthy boundaries: set app time limits preventing endless scrolling, practice comparison awareness (notice 'everyone's life is better than mine' thoughts and stop scrolling), avoid controversial comment debates (escalates without nuance), and remember posts are highlight reels not reality. Social media should enhance real relationships, not substitute for them—likes don't equal genuine connection, and the time spent scrolling could be spent in actual conversation.

title: Modern Social Etiquette — Navigating Digital and In-Person Connection - Quiz
